metadata.regulation: EU CBAM Regulation (EU) 2023/956 and Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/1773
metadata.source: EU Commission Implementing Acts, JRC Technical Reports on CBAM Default Values (2025)
metadata.description: Progressive markup schedule on default emission values for CBAM goods when actual installation-level data is not available. Default values represent the average emission intensity of the worst-performing 10% of installations in the EU for each product.
metadata.disclaimer: For actual CBAM filings, use the official default values published by the EU Commission in the Official Journal. Values herein are based on JRC technical assessments and may be updated.

transitional_period.notes: Transitional period. Reporting only, no financial obligations. Default values may be used without penalty. UN Comtrade-based estimates accepted as fallback.

definitive_year_1.notes: First year of definitive period. CBAM certificates must be purchased. Commission-published default values used at face value. Importers should transition to actual data.

definitive_year_2.notes: 10% markup on default values to incentivize actual data reporting from installations. Importers relying on defaults face higher certificate costs.

definitive_year_3.notes: 20% markup on default values. Strong incentive to obtain verified installation-level data.

definitive_year_4_plus.notes: 30% maximum markup on default values. Applies from 2029 onwards. Importers should have supplier-specific data by this point.

clinker.source: JRC 2025 - Based on worst-performing 10% of EU clinker kilns
clinker.notes: Direct emissions dominated by CaCO3 calcination (~65%). Kiln fuel combustion ~35%.

grey_portland_cement.source: JRC 2025 - Weighted by typical 70% clinker ratio
grey_portland_cement.notes: Lower than clinker due to dilution with SCMs (slag, fly ash, limestone). Clinker ratio varies 65-95%.

white_portland_cement.source: JRC 2025 - Higher energy intensity due to purity requirements
white_portland_cement.notes: White cement requires higher kiln temperature and purer raw materials, ~12% more energy-intensive.

aluminous_cement.source: JRC 2025 - Based on bauxite-limestone fusion process
aluminous_cement.notes: Lower process CO2 than Portland clinker (no CaCO3 calcination at same scale) but higher energy.

other_hydraulic_cements.source: JRC 2025 - Category average for blended/composite cements
other_hydraulic_cements.notes: Includes slag cement, pozzolanic cement, composite cements. Lower clinker factor.

pig_iron.notes: BF-BOF route. Coke consumption is primary emission source.

crude_steel_bof.notes: Integrated route including sintering, coking, blast furnace, and basic oxygen furnace.

crude_steel_eaf.notes: EAF with 100% scrap. Indirect emissions significant due to electricity consumption (~400 kWh/t). Total depends heavily on grid mix.

sintered_ore.notes: Sinter strand emissions from coke breeze combustion and limestone calcination.

iron_castings.notes: Cupola and induction furnace based. High indirect emissions from electricity.

ferro_alloys.source: JRC 2025 - Weighted average across ferro-alloy types
ferro_alloys.notes: Very high electricity consumption (4,000-10,000 kWh/t). FeSi is most electricity-intensive.

hot_rolled_flat_products.notes: Includes embedded emissions from steelmaking plus rolling energy. BF-BOF assumed.

steel_tubes_pipes.source: JRC 2025 - Seamless and welded tube production
steel_tubes_pipes.notes: Includes upstream steelmaking emissions plus tube forming/welding energy.

unwrought_aluminium_primary.notes: Extremely electricity-intensive (~14.5 MWh/t). Total depends heavily on grid mix. Direct = anode consumption + PFC. Indirect = smelter electricity.

unwrought_aluminium_alloyed.notes: Slightly higher direct emissions due to alloying furnace energy.

aluminium_plates_sheets.notes: Upstream smelting plus hot/cold rolling energy.

aluminium_foil.notes: Additional energy for thin-gauge rolling. Highest processing energy in aluminium downstream.

aluminium_wire.notes: Upstream smelting plus wire drawing energy.

ammonia_anhydrous.notes: Steam methane reforming (SMR) route. 1.2 tCO2 process + ~0.9 tCO2 fuel. CCS could reduce by 90%.

ammonia_in_solution.source: JRC 2025 - Adjusted for water content (~20-25% NH3)
ammonia_in_solution.notes: Lower per tonne due to dilution. Expressed per tonne of solution, not per tonne NH3.

nitric_acid.source: JRC 2025 - Includes upstream ammonia + N2O from oxidation
nitric_acid.notes: N2O from catalytic oxidation is major source. Modern abatement reduces N2O by >95%. Default assumes partial abatement.

urea.source: JRC 2025 - Net of CO2 chemically bound in urea
urea.notes: Ammonia production CO2 minus CO2 sequestered in urea molecule (~0.73 tCO2/t urea). Net direct emissions lower than ammonia.

ammonium_nitrate.source: JRC 2025 - Includes upstream ammonia + nitric acid
ammonium_nitrate.notes: Combines ammonia and nitric acid emissions. N2O emissions from nitric acid production are significant.

mixed_fertilisers_npk.source: JRC 2025 - Weighted by typical NPK ratio and precursors
mixed_fertilisers_npk.notes: Varies significantly by N:P:K ratio. Default based on representative 15-15-15 formulation.

calcium_ammonium_nitrate.source: JRC 2025 - AN diluted with calcium carbonate
calcium_ammonium_nitrate.notes: 27% N content typical. Lower intensity per tonne than pure AN due to dilution with limestone.

electricity.notes: Default for direct interconnection imports. Country-specific grid factors should be used where available. No indirect emissions for electricity as a final product.

hydrogen_smr.notes: Conventional SMR without CCS. ~9-11 tCO2/tH2 depending on energy efficiency.

hydrogen_smr_ccs.notes: Blue hydrogen with CCS. Residual emissions from incomplete capture, upstream methane leakage not included in direct.

hydrogen_electrolysis.source: JRC 2025 - Grid-average electricity; 0 if certified renewable
hydrogen_electrolysis.notes: ~50-55 MWh/tH2. Zero direct emissions. Indirect depends entirely on electricity source. Default assumes mixed grid. Green hydrogen (certified renewable) = 0 tCO2e.

hydrogen_atr.notes: ATR route typically has better CCS integration potential. Without CCS, similar to SMR.

calculation_method_hierarchy.description: Verified emission data from the specific installation producing the goods, based on direct monitoring and measurement.
calculation_method_hierarchy.notes: Preferred method. Requires installation-level monitoring per EU CBAM MRV requirements.

calculation_method_hierarchy.description: Installation emission data reported by the operator but not yet independently verified.
calculation_method_hierarchy.notes: Acceptable during transition. Must be verified for definitive period claims.

calculation_method_hierarchy.description: Average emission intensity for the product in the country of production, published by the EU Commission or derived from reliable national statistics.
calculation_method_hierarchy.notes: Used when installation data unavailable. Based on IEA, national inventory, or industry association data.

calculation_method_hierarchy.description: Commission-published default values based on the worst-performing 10% of EU installations, subject to annual markup per the schedule above.
calculation_method_hierarchy.notes: Fallback method. Markup incentivizes migration to actual data. Default = p90 EU emissions.

calculation_method_hierarchy.description: Estimation based on trade statistics and average emission intensities. Only allowed during the transitional period (2023-2025).
calculation_method_hierarchy.notes: Transitional period only. Not accepted from 2026 onwards.

indirect_emissions_methodology.description: Rules for calculating indirect (Scope 2) embedded emissions in CBAM goods
rules: Indirect emissions are included for all CBAM goods except electricity (which is a final product)
The electricity emission factor should reflect the actual source of electricity used by the installation
If actual source data unavailable, use the country-specific grid emission factor
If country grid factor unavailable, use the default (0.800 tCO2e/MWh)
Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) for renewable electricity may be used to reduce indirect emissions to zero, subject to additionality and temporal matching criteria
